cards.front: What are the three components of a for loop header?
cards.back:
1) Init-statement (initialization)
2) Condition (tested before each iteration)
3) End-expression (executed after each iteration)

cards.text:
A typical for loop counting 0 to 9:
{{c1::for (int i = 0; i < 10; ++i)}}
{
    std::cout << i;
}

cards.front: In what order are the for loop components executed?
cards.back:
1) Init-statement (once at start)
2) Condition check (before each iteration)
3) Loop body (if condition true)
4) End-expression (after each iteration)
Then back to step 2

cards.question: What does this code print?
cards.explanation: Loop starts at 0, continues while i < 5, and increments after each iteration. Prints 0, 1, 2, 3, 4.

cards.front: What is the scope of a variable declared in a for loop's init-statement?
cards.back: The variable is scoped to the for loop only - it exists from declaration until the end of the loop and is then destroyed.

cards.question: Will this compile?
cards.code:
for (int i = 0; i < 5; ++i)
{
    std::cout << i;
}
std::cout << i; // outside loop
cards.answer: No, variable i is out of scope after the for loop ends.
cards.explanation: Variables declared in the init-statement have loop scope only. After the loop ends, i is destroyed and cannot be accessed.

cards.text: It's best practice to use the prefix {{c1::++i}} rather than postfix i++ in for loops, as it may be more efficient.

cards.front: When should you use a for loop instead of a while loop?
cards.back: When you know how many iterations you need in advance, especially when using a counter variable. For loops keep all loop control in one place.

cards.text: An infinite for loop can be written as: {{c1::for (;;)}} { }

cards.front: Are the init-statement, condition, and end-expression required in a for loop?
cards.back: No, all three are optional. You can omit any or all (but the semicolons are still required): for (;;) is an infinite loop.

cards.explanation:
Follow these practices for clean for loops:
  • Use for loops when iteration count is known
  • Declare loop counter in init-statement (int i = 0)
  • Use prefix increment (++i) instead of postfix (i++)
  • Name counters i, j, k for simple loops; use descriptive names for complex loops
  • Keep loop counters simple - avoid complex end-expressions
  • Don't modify the loop counter inside the loop body
examples:
// Good: simple and clear
for (int i = 0; i < 10; ++i) { }

// Bad: modifying counter in body
for (int i = 0; i < 10; ++i) { ++i; }

cards.question: What does this code print?
cards.explanation: Loop starts at 5, continues while i > 0, and decrements after each iteration. Counts down from 5 to 1.

cards.front: When is the end-expression in a for loop evaluated?
cards.back: After the loop body executes, before the next condition check. It's executed once per iteration (except the first).

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Semicolon after for creates a null statement. The block is not part of the loop and i is out of scope.
cards.explanation: The semicolon makes the loop body empty. The block below is not part of the loop, and i is out of scope there. This is a common and subtle bug.
